title: Semantic Integrity Matters: Benchmarking and Preserving High-Density Reasoning in KV Cache Compression
abstract: While Key-Value (KV) cache compression is essential for efficient LLM inference, current evaluations disproportionately focus on \textbf{sparse retrieval} tasks, potentially masking the degradation of High-Density Reasoning where Chain-of-Thought (CoT) coherence is critical. We introduce KVFundaBench to systematically evaluate this gap, revealing a sharp dichotomy: while retrieval tasks remain robust, reasoning tasks exhibit severe Task-Dependent Degradation under aggressive compression due to disrupted CoT links. Extending our analysis to the DeepSeek-R1 model, we uncover that its specialized attention patterns offer unique insights into the fragility of reasoning chains. Guided by these findings—specifically the necessity of preserving few-shot examples as indivisible \textbf{Semantic Units}—we propose ShotKV. This approach explicitly separates prefill and decoding phases to prioritize semantic integrity. Empirical results demonstrate that ShotKV achieves 9\%-18\% accuracy improvements on long-context generation tasks and effectively generalizes to document QA, all while delivering an 11\% latency reduction compared to full cache inference.
decision_comment:
Most existing KV cache compression methods are primarily evaluated in retrieval tasks which are inherently sparse. However, many real-world applications, particularly reasoning, require modeling much denser signals. This paper introduces KVFundaBench, a collection of existing tasks spanning both knowledge and reasoning tasks, and shows that attention patterns in these settings differ substantially from those observed in sparse retrieval scenarios. Based on these findings, the authors argue that preserving few-shot examples as indivisible semantic units is crucial, and propose ShotKV, a method that separates prefill and decoding phases to better maintain semantic integrity. Empirically, ShotKV achieves 9-18% accuracy improvements on long-context generalization tasks, while also reducing latency by 11% compared to using the full KV cache.

Reviewers agree that the paper (the problem formulation, benchmark, and method) is well-motivated (KqJh, WRpj, EFFb, h9vf), empirical results and analysis are comprehensive and compelling (KqJh, h9vf), and the writing/presentation is clear (KqJh).

Weakness raised by reviewers include: (1) attention score aggregation doesn’t consider semantic importance (KqJh), (2) characteristics of some existing work is a bit misleading , e.g., LongBench (WRpj), (3) some experimental design can be improved, e.g., considering data contamination (WRpj), (4) in some settings (e.g., arithmetic reasoning), the gains from the proposed method is minimal (WRpj), (5) presentation, e.g., inconsistent or unclear terminologies, analysis results (EFFb), (6) missing comparison to KV quantization methods (h9vf), and (7) reliance on few-shot structure in the prompt (h9vf). That said, most of these concerns were addressed during the rebuttal, with reviewers explicitly acknowledging satisfactory clarification – authors are highly encouraged to incorporate these into the final version of the paper.

Regarding the absence of additional model families (EFFb), the AC considers the current coverage (spanning three model families) to be sufficiently comprehensive.

reviews.summary:
In this paper the authors observe that key-value (KV) compression methods, which are commonly used for reducing latency in large language models (LLMs), although very succesful for long-context retrieval tasks, they tend to cause a sharp drop in performance for high-density reasoning tasks. In order to systematically explore and quantify this observation, they introduce KVFundaBench, a new benchmark covering five fundamental capability categories: world knowledge, commonsense reasoning, arithmetic reasoning, code generation, and safety—along with long-context generation. Their experiments across multiple models and compression methods reveal strong task-dependent degradation: the performance of arithmetic reasoning is most sensitive to changing compression ratios while world knowledge and commonsense reasoning are fairly robust. Further analysis offers more insights, for example that multi-step reasoning models are more compression-resilient than instruction-tuned models or that shorter prompts are more vulnerable than longer ones. 

Based on these and towards mitigating reasoning degradation ShotKV is introduced: this is a strategy for compressing KV which (i) treats few-shot examples as indivisible semantic units during prefill (ii) separates prefill compression from a dynamic, token-level decoding compression strategy (computing attention-weight based scores for the shots and the tokens). Empirical results demonstrate that ShotKV achieves 9%-18% accuracy improvements on long-context generation task, generalizes to document QA, all while achieving  11% reduction in latency as compared to the full cache case.
reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses:
## Strengths

- Very interesting, complete and self-contained narrative: first (i) a problem with KV cache compression is clearly articulated, then (ii) a benchmark is assembled (KVFundaBench) to demonstrate it and finally (iii) an approach (ShotKV) for mitigating it is proposed and successfully tested.
- This paper includes extensive empirical evidence for the numerous claims in the text, with most of them directly reflecting simple, intuitive/insightful arguments that are easy to follow and reason about. Most importantly, empirical observations, also appear to "naturally" pave the path to the method to mitigate limitations.
- KVFundaBench is actually a rebranding/packaging of existing benchmarks/datasets, yet its presentation (Section 3.1) is excellent: the mapping of tasks to datasets to metrics is crystal clear and the description of comparison baselines, parameters and the evaluation enviroment, although succinct is fairly complete.
- ShotKV is based on two design principles that are both very simple and effective: (a) few-shot examples should be kept indivisible, (b) prefilling and decoding compression.  


## Weaknesses

- Studies in these paper assume access to KV cache. Although this is the case for open-weight models (like LLaMA, Mistral and DeepSeek ones) that are self-hosted, commercial deployments accessible through some API do not typically provide KV cache access. This means that KVFundaBench and ShotKV cannot be used in such commercial setups, and this is an important limitation for the applicability of this work in practice.
- In ShotKV, computation of scores (Equations (3) and (7)) is a function of attention weights which are model-dependent and can be noisy; also their aggregation does not map to semantic importance in a principled way. It follows that the definition of most influential tokens to preserve (during both prefill and decoding phases) is not expected to be particularly robust in different settings.
reviews.key_questions_for_authors:
## Questions
1. It would be interesting to further strengthen the suitability/robustness of the score functions. For example if some of the shots are modified towards being less "useful" (for example by adding noise to some of them or by keeping a part of a shot), then score functions for them should systematically give lower values. Is this indeed the case?
reviews.final_justification: I am retaining my positive score based on my overall impression from the original reviews, as well as the authors’ responses to them.

reviews.summary: The paper analyzes the impact of KV cache compression on LLMs, and finds that current methods only focus on sparse retrieval tasks and fail to capture the degradation of "High-Density Reasoning" capabilities. The authors introduce KVFundaBench, which includes world knowledge, commonsense, arithmetic, code, safety, and long-context generation, and report task and model dependent sensitivities to KV compression. Motivated by their observation, the authors present ShotKV, a training-free scheme that preserves whole shots during prefill and applies attention-driven token-level compression during decoding. ShotKV improves long-generation accuracy by 9–18% over baselines and reduces latency by 11%.
reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses:
Strengths:
* The paper identifies “High-Density Reasoning” and links failures to disrupted semantic continuity in CoT prompts.
* The design principles behind ShotKV are intuitive and easy to implement.

Weakness:
* The authors characterize LongBench as a "sparse retrieval" scenario. While this characterization applies to Needle-In-A-Haystack, it is an oversimplification of LongBench, which includes complex tasks such as multi-document QA, summarization, and code completion—tasks that also require reasoning and synthesizing information across long contexts.
* The paper does not provide a direct empirical comparison between a 4,000-token reasoning task and a 100k-token sparse retrieval task using the same compression methods to substantiate the claim that the former is "significantly harder".
* The authors present "Task-Dependent Degradation" as a key finding. However, the paper does not adequately address **data contamination**. It is probable that models rely on their internal parametric memory to answer WK/CSR questions, rather than actively utilizing the KV cache.
*  In evaluating ShotKV on many-shot Arithmetic Reasoning (table 4), the actual gap between the proposed method and the strongest existing baseline is small.
reviews.key_questions_for_authors:
* Can you provide an empirical comparison that directly contrasts a long-context sparse retrieval task (e.g., 100k tokens) with a high-density reasoning task (e.g., 4k tokens) under the same compression algorithms.
* Given that Table 4 shows ShotKV only outperforms ChunkKV by ~1.05% at a 10% compression ratio, could you clarify the significance of ShotKV over other methods?
reviews.final_justification: My concerns are addressed during the rebuttal. Overall, the idea of links failures to disrupted semantic continuity in CoT prompts is novel and I would recommend this paper.

reviews.summary: The authors identify a gap in the current evaluation of Key-Value (KV) cache compression methods: the disproportionate reliance on sparse retrieval tasks (e.g., Needle-In-A-Haystack), which fails to capture the degradation of models on tasks requiring logical coherence . To address this, the paper introduces KVFundaBench to systematically evaluate "High-Density Reasoning" tasks under compression. Based on their empirical findings, the authors propose ShotKV, a method that separates prefill and decoding compression phases and preserves few-shot examples as indivisible semantic units, which successfully mitigates some performance loss under aggressive KV compression.
reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses:
**Soundness: Poor**
* **Strengths:** The paper identifies that current KV cache compression evaluations over-index on sparse retrieval tasks, which masks the true fragility of compressed models. The introduction of KVFundaBench and the resulting ShotKV method is a logical progression.
* **Weaknesses:**
    * **Superficial Analysis:** The core premise lacks deep experimental validation. For example, Figure 1 is presented to illustrate the performance gap between long-context and arithmetic benchmarks, yet it is never explicitly referenced or analyzed in the main text.
    * **Missing Baselines (Models):** The evaluation uses LLaMA-3.1, Mistral, and DeepSeek-R1. Conspicuously absent is the Qwen series, which is a state-of-the-art standard model. This raises concerns about whether the findings generalize.
    * **Incomplete Efficiency Metrics:** In Table 5, ShotKV's latency and throughput are only compared against FullKV. A proper evaluation requires comparisons against the other compression baselines. Furthermore, the specific computational overhead of calculating and sorting attention scores for Semantic Units during the prefill phase is not properly quantified.
    * **Missing Sparse Retrieval Validation:** Real-world applications mix reasoning and retrieval. The authors should be evaluate ShotKV on sparse retrieval tasks to ensure their method does not catastrophically degrade general retrieval capabilities.

**Presentation: Poor**
* **Weaknesses:**
    * **Concept Overload & Disjointed Narrative:** The paper introduces heavy terminology (e.g., "High-Density Reasoning", "Semantic Integrity") without effectively operationalizing or connecting them. The narrative feels disjointed and lacks proper polishing.

**Significance: Fair**
* **Strengths:** Highlighting and attempting to resolve the flaws in current evaluation metrics is a highly relevant problem for the community.
* **Weaknesses:** The practical impact is limited by the shallow empirical analysis and the methodologically incomplete validation of ShotKV.

**Originality: Fair**
* **Strengths:** Shifting the compression paradigm from token-level dropping to preserving the semantic integrity of indivisible “Semantic Units” (via few-shot examples) is reasonable.
reviews.key_questions_for_authors: The manuscript requires thorough structural reorganization and editorial polishing to improve its narrative clarity, alongside significantly more comprehensive and in-depth experimental analyses to rigorously substantiate its core claims.
reviews.final_justification: The reviewer's final response has addressed my biggest concern, so I have raised my score. However, as I am unfamiliar with some pieces of related work, I have lowered my confidence score accordingly.

reviews.summary: This paper introduces the KVFundaBench and systematically reveals a key differentiated phenomenon in KV cache compression: while traditional retrieval tasks remain relatively robust under compression, “high-density reasoning” tasks involving chain-of-thought (CoT) coherence suffer from severe task-dependent performance degradation under aggressive compression due to the disruption of semantic reasoning chains. To mitigate this issue, the authors propose ShotKV, whose core idea is to treat the few-shot examples in prompts as indivisible semantic units and preserve them intact. Furthermore, the method adopts explicitly separated compression strategies for the prefill and decoding stages.
reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses:
Strength:

(1) The paper presents a clear motivation, and the experimental setup is well-designed.
(2) The analysis based on KVFundaBench is comprehensive.
(3) The experimental results demonstrate that the proposed method achieves significant improvements.

Weakness:

See the Key Questions for Authors.
reviews.key_questions_for_authors:
(1) In the Prefill Compression stage, ChunkKV performs sorting within the window, whereas the proposed method only performs sorting within the n-shot examples. Moreover, the method relies on Observation 4, which has already been demonstrated in ChunkKV. During the decoding phase, the proposed approach relies on attention-score-based ranking, which resembles an incremental extension of H2O. The authors should therefore more clearly articulate and emphasize the key innovations of their approach.

(2) The paper should include comparative experiments with KV quantization methods. In addition, ablation studies on prefill compression ratios and decoding compression ratios are currently missing.

(3) The proposed method appears to rely heavily on the presence of a clear few-shot structure in the prompt. For zero-shot settings or unstructured long-text scenarios (e.g., long-document summarization), the paper only provides preliminary experimental results. This raises concerns regarding the method’s applicability in practical deployments. Furthermore, while Table 4 presents results under different compression ratios, Table 3 only reports results at a 10% compression ratio, which limits the comprehensiveness of the evaluation.

(4) While I understand the page limit constraints, the main paper should still include a Related Work section that briefly discusses the most closely related studies, with additional details provided in the appendix if necessary. Moving the entire Related Work section to the appendix is not an appropriate practice.

I will raise my score if the author addresses my issue.
reviews.final_justification: The authors have addressed my main concerns. However, I still hope that, as stated, they will reorganize the introduction and method sections in the final main paper to more clearly highlight the novelty of the approach, and also add a related work section.
